narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after pouring your heart into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our predictive model saw your path to the bar exam as riskier than we can take on—it's not about you as a person, but the patterns it spotted in your profile that echo too many stories of struggle. At the top of what weighed heaviest was your Year 3 decile of 1.0, placing you at the bottom rung academically in your school, which the model flagged as the strongest signal pulling toward bar failure, as it often does for students who couldn't climb higher by the end. Right behind that was your Year 1 decile of 1.0, reinforcing a consistent early challenge that made the model worry about building momentum over time. Your LSAT score of 28.0 added to that concern, standing out as another key factor tilting the odds downward, since it suggests the analytical demands of bar prep might prove tough. Even your middle family income bracket played a role, as the model links it to added pressures without the buffers higher incomes sometimes provide. Your Asian background factored in too, unfortunately aligning in the data with heightened hurdles toward passing. Your undergrad GPA of 2.5 continued this theme, painting a picture of grades that haven't yet shown the lift needed for bar success. Going full-time nudged things further toward risk, as the model sees relentless demands without part-time flexibility as a common stumble point. The one bright spot was your gender, as female students in the patterns often edge toward passing, but it wasn't quite enough to counterbalance the rest. Together, these pieces formed a mosaic the model couldn't ignore—a 55.3% predicted chance of bar failure that means we have to say no, even as we root for you to defy it. You've got target_law written in your stars, and with targeted steps like boosting study strategies or retaking the LSAT, you could rewrite this story. Keep pushing; law needs voices like yours.
